questions.question: Explain the difference between a process and a thread with suitable examples.
keyPoints: Process is an executing program with separate memory space
Thread is a lightweight execution unit within a process
Threads share code, data, and OS resources; processes don't
Example: Browser (process) having multiple tabs (threads)

questions.question: What is Process Control Block (PCB)? List any four information stored in PCB.
keyPoints: PCB is a data structure maintaining process information
Contains: Process ID, State, Program Counter, CPU Registers
Also stores: Memory management info, I/O status, Accounting data
Enables context switching by storing complete process state

questions.question: Describe the five basic process states and explain the possible state transitions.
keyPoints: States: New, Ready, Running, Waiting, Terminated
Ready→Running: Scheduler dispatches process
Running→Ready: Preemption by scheduler
Running→Waiting: Process waits for I/O/event
Waiting→Ready: I/O completion or event occurs
Running→Terminated: Process completes execution

questions.question: What is context switching? Explain its role in process management.
keyPoints: Context switching is saving/restoring CPU state of processes
Involves storing current process PCB and loading next process PCB
Enables multiprogramming and time-sharing
Adds overhead - reduces actual CPU utilization time

questions.question: Explain the difference between preemptive and non-preemptive CPU scheduling.
keyPoints: Preemptive: OS can forcibly take CPU from running process
Non-preemptive: Process runs to completion or voluntary yield
Preemptive provides better response time but needs synchronization
Examples: Round Robin (preemptive), FCFS (non-preemptive)

questions.question: What is Interprocess Communication (IPC)? Mention any two methods of IPC.
keyPoints: IPC allows processes to communicate and share data
Methods: Shared Memory, Message Queues, Pipes, Signals, Sockets
Shared Memory is fastest - direct memory access
Message Passing involves kernel-mediated data transfer

questions.question: Explain the concept of deadlock in process management.
keyPoints: Deadlock occurs when processes wait for resources held by each other
Four necessary conditions: Mutual Exclusion, Hold and Wait
No Preemption, Circular Wait
Can be prevented by breaking any necessary condition

questions.question: Discuss the various CPU scheduling algorithms. Compare FCFS, SJF, and Round Robin algorithms with respect to waiting time, throughput, and convoy effect.
keyPoints: FCFS: Simple but causes convoy effect, long waiting for short processes
SJF: Optimal average waiting time but requires knowledge of burst times, causes starvation
Round Robin: Good for time-sharing, provides fair CPU allocation
Comparison: SJF has minimum avg wait, FCFS has convoy effect, RR has balanced performance
Time quantum affects RR effectiveness - too small increases context switches, too large becomes FCFS

questions.question: Explain the process creation mechanism in operating systems. What is the purpose of fork() and exec() system calls? How do parent and child processes differ?
keyPoints: Process creation: OS allocates PCB, allocates memory, loads program, sets up context
fork() creates new process by duplicating parent - returns 0 to child, PID to parent
exec() replaces current process image with new program - doesn't create new process
Child gets copy of parent's address space but has unique PID
Parent and child: Can share file descriptors, different memory (copy-on-write)
Parent typically waits for child using wait() system call

questions.question: What is process synchronization? Explain the Critical Section problem and its solutions.
keyPoints: Synchronization coordinates concurrent access to shared resources
Critical Section: Code segment accessing shared variables
Requirements: Mutual Exclusion, Progress, Bounded Waiting
Software solutions: Peterson's Algorithm, Test-and-Set
Hardware solutions: Atomic instructions, Memory Barriers
Synchronization primitives: Mutex, Semaphore, Monitor
Semaphore: Integer variable with atomic wait() and signal() operations

questions.question: Explain the concept of threads. Differentiate between user-level threads and kernel-level threads. Discuss advantages and disadvantages of multithreading.
keyPoints: Thread: Lightweight process, smallest CPU execution unit
User-level threads: Managed by thread library, kernel unaware, fast context switch
Kernel-level threads: Managed by OS kernel, slower but can utilize multiprocessors
Multithreading advantages: Responsiveness, resource sharing, economy, scalability
Disadvantages: Complexity, synchronization needed, debugging difficult
Examples: Java threads (can be either), Windows Win32 (kernel), POSIX Pthreads (both)
